raw:
{
  "weights": {
    "R1.1": 0.7,
    "R1.2": 0.7,
    "R1.3": 0.3,
    "R1.4": 0.3,
    "R1.5": 0.7,
    "R1.6": 1.0,
    "R1.7": 0.3,
    "RX.1": 1.0,
    "RX.2": 1.0,
    "RX.3": 1.0,
    "RX.4": 1.0,
    "RX.5": 1.0
  },
  "active_set": [
    "R1.1",
    "R1.2",
    "R1.5",
    "R1.6",
    "RX.1",
    "RX.2",
    "RX.3",
    "RX.4",
    "RX.5"
  ],
  "category_focus": "R1",
  "clarification_needed": true,
  "clarification_question": "Which scope should I prioritize: (A) sentence- and paragraph-level writing polish only (no content or structure changes), (B) substantive conceptual and structural revision including reframing the contribution and tightening the logic, or (C) scholarship/novelty positioning and claim-risk review (e.g., tempering “first” claims and suggesting citations)?",
  "rationale": {
    "R1.1": "Improving a research draft commonly requires refining the core thesis, gap statement, and contribution framing.",
    "R1.2": "Strengthening the logical chain from definitions to conclusions is central to a high-quality revision.",
    "R1.5": "Section- and subsection-level reordering can materially improve clarity and reader guidance beyond sentence edits.",
    "R1.6": "Polishing grammar, concision, and tone is a load-bearing part of making the draft more readable and professional.",
    "RX.1": "Clear disclosure of uncertainties (e.g., assumptions and scope limits) is necessary when proposing revisions.",
    "RX.2": "Edits must avoid overstating claims such as being the 'first' without robust support.",
    "RX.3": "The work should remain within the scope of improving the provided text without introducing unsupported content or analyses.",
    "RX.4": "Revisions should avoid encouraging interpretations that could mislead downstream scientific use.",
    "RX.5": "Any guidance must distinguish what comes from the provided draft versus external assumptions to maintain traceability."
  }
}

parsed.clarification_question: Which scope should I prioritize: (A) sentence- and paragraph-level writing polish only (no content or structure changes), (B) substantive conceptual and structural revision including reframing the contribution and tightening the logic, or (C) scholarship/novelty positioning and claim-risk review (e.g., tempering “first” claims and suggesting citations)?

rationale.R1.1: Improving a research draft commonly requires refining the core thesis, gap statement, and contribution framing.
rationale.R1.2: Strengthening the logical chain from definitions to conclusions is central to a high-quality revision.
rationale.R1.5: Section- and subsection-level reordering can materially improve clarity and reader guidance beyond sentence edits.
rationale.R1.6: Polishing grammar, concision, and tone is a load-bearing part of making the draft more readable and professional.
rationale.RX.1: Clear disclosure of uncertainties (e.g., assumptions and scope limits) is necessary when proposing revisions.
rationale.RX.2: Edits must avoid overstating claims such as being the 'first' without robust support.
rationale.RX.3: The work should remain within the scope of improving the provided text without introducing unsupported content or analyses.
rationale.RX.4: Revisions should avoid encouraging interpretations that could mislead downstream scientific use.
rationale.RX.5: Any guidance must distinguish what comes from the provided draft versus external assumptions to maintain traceability.
